subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look around you for a moment. If you are sitting near a window, you might see a acacia tree waving in the wind, a lizard sunning itself on a warm rock, a goat chewing, or a flamingo wading in a shallow lake. You might also see stones, a plastic bottle, a metal spoon and the phone or tablet you are learning from. Some of these things are alive and some have never been alive. In this unit you will learn how scientists tell the difference, and then how they sort the enormous variety of living things into neat, useful groups.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 7 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
List and describe the seven characteristics shared by all living organisms.
Decide whether an object is living, non-living, or was once alive, giving reasons.
Describe the main groups (kingdoms) into which living things are classified.
Explain the difference between vertebrates and invertebrates and give local examples of each.
Describe the structure of a virus and discuss why scientists argue about whether viruses are alive.
Define the word 'species' and explain how scientists decide if two organisms belong to the same species.
Use a dichotomous key to identify an organism, working through it step by step.
Construct your own dichotomous key to identify a small set of local animals, leaves or objects.
Study this table before you read on. These words appear again and again throughout the unit, so learning them early will make everything easier.

concepts.title: Grouping and Identifying Organisms — part 1
concepts.explanation: Look around you for a moment. If you are sitting near a window, you might see a acacia tree waving in the wind, a lizard sunning itself on a warm rock, a goat chewing, or a flamingo wading in a shallow lake. You might also see stones, a plastic bottle, a metal spoon and the phone or tablet you are learning from. Some of these things are alive and some have never been alive. In this unit you will learn how scientists tell the difference, and then how they sort the enormous variety of living things into neat, useful…
concepts.example: Scientists have described more than one and a half million kinds of living things, and they believe millions more are still waiting to be discovered. East Africa alone is home to lions, elephants, giraffes, camels,…

concepts.title: Grouping and Identifying Organisms — part 2
concepts.explanation: Scientists have described more than one and a half million kinds of living things, and they believe millions more are still waiting to be discovered. East Africa alone is home to lions, elephants, giraffes, camels, tilapia in the Jubba River, coral fish along the Indian Ocean coast, and countless insects, plants and tiny microbes. Without a way of grouping and naming all these organisms, biology would be a hopeless muddle. Classification is the tool that turns the muddle into order.
concepts.example: By the end of this unit you will be able to name the seven signs of life, decide whether a strange object is living or non-living, understand what a species is, explain why viruses puzzle scientists, and build your very…

concepts.title: Grouping and Identifying Organisms — part 3
concepts.explanation: By the end of this unit you will be able to name the seven signs of life, decide whether a strange object is living or non-living, understand what a species is, explain why viruses puzzle scientists, and build your very own identification key for the animals and plants around your home. This skill matters in real life too: doctors classify the germs that cause malaria, measles and COVID-19; farmers classify their livestock and crops; and conservationists classify wildlife to decide which species need protecting.…
concepts.example: Explain the difference between vertebrates and invertebrates and give local examples of each.

concepts.example: Look around you for a moment. If you are sitting near a window, you might see a acacia tree waving in the wind, a lizard sunning itself on a warm rock, a goat chewing, or a flamingo wading in a shallow lake. You might…

explorations.title: Living, Non-living or Once-living?
explorations.context: To sort objects from around your home into three groups - living, non-living, and once-living - using the seven characteristics of life.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down: Which do you think will be the hardest object to classify, and why? For example, will a wooden spoon or a piece of canjeero be tricky to place?
explorations.answer: Which objects were the easiest to classify? Which were the hardest? Why? The wooden spoon and the cloth are once-living. What living things did they come from? Did the potted plant show all seven characteristics? How do you know it feeds, even though you never see it eat?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To sort objects from around your home into three groups - living, non-living, and once-living - using the seven characteristics of life.

explorations.title: A Leaf Hunt and Sorting Challenge
explorations.context: To collect several different leaves from around your home and sort them into groups based on features you can observe - the first step towards building a key.
explorations.prompt: Write down: What is the single most useful feature for telling leaves apart - shape, size, edge, or vein pattern? Explain your guess before you begin.
explorations.answer: Which feature separated your leaves most cleanly? Was your hypothesis correct? Was any feature hard to decide 'yes or no' for? Why is that a problem for a good key? Could a friend follow your splits and reach the same groups? If not, how could you make the questions clearer?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To collect several different leaves from around your home and sort them into groups based on features you can observe - the first step towards building a key.

explorations.title: Build Your Own Dichotomous Key
explorations.context: To construct a working dichotomous key that another person can use to identify four local insects or four leaves.
explorations.prompt: Write down: How many pairs of statements do you think you will need to tell four organisms apart? (Hint: think about how each question splits the group in two.)
explorations.answer: How many pairs of statements did you actually need? Was your hypothesis correct? Give your key to a family member and ask them to identify one specimen. Did it work? Which of your questions caused confusion? Rewrite it to be clearer.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To construct a working dichotomous key that another person can use to identify four local insects or four leaves.

explorations.title: Modelling a Virus
explorations.context: To make a simple model of a virus so you can understand and remember its structure.
explorations.prompt: Write down: Which part of your model represents the part that lets the virus enter a cell?
explorations.answer: What is the job of the spikes on a real virus? Your model cannot move, feed or grow on its own. How does this help explain why scientists argue about whether viruses are alive? A virus can only make copies of itself inside a living cell. Why does this make hand-washing and vaccination such powerful…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To make a simple model of a virus so you can understand and remember its structure.

methods.title: Living, Non-living or Once-living?
methods.example: To sort objects from around your home into three groups - living, non-living, and once-living - using the seven characteristics of life.
steps: Draw a table with three columns: Living, Non-living (never alive), and Once-living.
Take each object in turn. Ask: does it show all seven signs of life right now?
If it shows all seven, place it in Living. If it never had life, place it in Non-living.
If it does not show the signs now but was once part of a living thing (like wood or cloth), place it in Once-living.
For each object, write one reason for your choice.

methods.title: A Leaf Hunt and Sorting Challenge
methods.example: To collect several different leaves from around your home and sort them into groups based on features you can observe - the first step towards building a key.
steps: Lay all your leaves side by side on a clean surface.
Choose one feature - for example, 'Is the leaf long and narrow?' - and split the leaves into two piles: yes and no.
For each pile, choose another feature and split again.
Keep going until every leaf is separated from all the others.
Record which feature you used at each split.

methods.title: Build Your Own Dichotomous Key
methods.example: To construct a working dichotomous key that another person can use to identify four local insects or four leaves.
steps: Observe all four specimens carefully and list the features of each (number of legs, wings, size, colour, body shape).
Find one feature that splits the four into two groups of two. Write it as statement 1a and 1b.
For each group of two, find a feature that separates them. Write these as statements 2 and 3.
Each statement must end either in 'go to ...' or in the name of the organism.
Write your finished key neatly using numbered pairs.

methods.title: Modelling a Virus
methods.example: To make a simple model of a virus so you can understand and remember its structure.
steps: Form the dough or paper into a round ball. This is the protein coat.
Before closing the ball, tuck a few short threads inside. These are the RNA instructions.
Push the toothpicks or twigs gently into the outside of the ball to make the spikes.
Look at your model. Label a drawing of it in your notebook: protein coat, RNA, spikes.

workedExamples.title: Using the Key Step by Step
workedExamples.prompt: Let us identify the dragonfly using the numbered key above. We will show every step so you can see exactly how a key is used. Imagine you are holding the whole animal and can look at it carefully.
workedExamples.solution: Start at step 1. Look at the dragonfly. Does it have legs? Yes. Statement 1a says 'It has legs - go to 2.' So we go to step 2. Step 2. Does it have exactly six legs? Yes, like all insects it has six legs. Statement 2a says 'It has exactly six legs - go to 4.' So we go to step 4. Step 4. Does it have four wings? Yes, a dragonfly has four wings. Statement 4a says 'It has four wings - dragonfly.' We have arrived at the answer: dragonfly. The path we followed was 1a, 2a, 4a.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Breaking down food inside cells to release energy is called: (a) excretion (b) respiration (c) nutrition (d) growth
workedExamples.solution: (b) respiration. Respiration releases energy from food inside every cell.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is an invertebrate? (a) tilapia (b) camel (c) mosquito (d) flamingo
workedExamples.solution: (c) mosquito. A mosquito has no backbone, so it is an invertebrate. The others are vertebrates.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: A virus's outer coat is made of: (a) cellulose (b) protein (c) glass (d) water
workedExamples.solution: (b) protein. The outer coat of a virus is made of protein, with spikes on it.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Getting rid of waste made inside the body is called: (a) nutrition (b) sensitivity (c) excretion (d) reproduction
workedExamples.solution: (c) excretion. Excretion is removing waste made inside the body, such as carbon dioxide.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which instrument is needed to see a virus? (a) a hand lens (b) a school light microscope (c) an electron microscope (d) a telescope
workedExamples.solution: (c) an electron microscope. Viruses are far too small for a school light microscope.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: A group of organisms that can reproduce to make fertile offspring is a: (a) kingdom (b) species (c) class (d) key
workedExamples.solution: (b) species. A species is defined by the ability to produce fertile offspring together.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The offspring of a lion and a tiger (a liger) is: (a) fertile (b) infertile (c) a new species (d) a vertebrate without a backbone
workedExamples.solution: (b) infertile. Ligers are healthy but cannot have young, so they are infertile.

practice.prompt: How many characteristics of life do all living organisms share? (a) three (b) five (c) seven (d) ten
practice.answer: (c) seven. All living things share the seven MRS GREN characteristics.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Breaking down food inside cells to release energy is called: (a) excretion (b) respiration (c) nutrition (d) growth
practice.answer: (b) respiration. Respiration releases energy from food inside every cell.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.answer: (c) mosquito. A mosquito has no backbone, so it is an invertebrate. The others are vertebrates.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A virus's outer coat is made of: (a) cellulose (b) protein (c) glass (d) water
practice.answer: (b) protein. The outer coat of a virus is made of protein, with spikes on it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Getting rid of waste made inside the body is called: (a) nutrition (b) sensitivity (c) excretion (d) reproduction
practice.answer: (c) excretion. Excretion is removing waste made inside the body, such as carbon dioxide.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which instrument is needed to see a virus? (a) a hand lens (b) a school light microscope (c) an electron microscope (d) a telescope
practice.answer: (c) an electron microscope. Viruses are far too small for a school light microscope.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A group of organisms that can reproduce to make fertile offspring is a: (a) kingdom (b) species (c) class (d) key
practice.answer: (b) species. A species is defined by the ability to produce fertile offspring together.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The offspring of a lion and a tiger (a liger) is: (a) fertile (b) infertile (c) a new species (d) a vertebrate without a backbone
practice.answer: (b) infertile. Ligers are healthy but cannot have young, so they are infertile.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: 'Dichotomous' means: (a) very small (b) branching into two (c) made of cells (d) living in water
practice.answer: (b) branching into two. At each step a dichotomous key offers exactly two choices.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is the best question for a dichotomous key? (a) Is it pretty? (b) Is it big? (c) Does it have six legs? (d) Is it interesting?
practice.answer: (c) Does it have six legs? It has a clear yes-or-no answer everyone would agree on.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Green plants get their nutrition by: (a) eating animals (b) photosynthesis (c) breathing (d) replication
practice.answer: (b) photosynthesis. Green plants make their own food using sunlight, water and air.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which kingdom does a mushroom belong to? (a) plants (b) animals (c) fungi (d) bacteria
practice.answer: (c) fungi. Mushrooms belong to the fungi kingdom; they do not photosynthesise.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Living, Non-living or Once-living?
activities.materials: A notebook and pencil; About ten safe objects you can gather: a stone, a plastic cup, a metal spoon, a wooden spoon, a piece of cloth, a leaf, a potted plant, a piece of dried date, a feather, a small potted seedling
steps: Draw a table with three columns: Living, Non-living (never alive), and Once-living.
Take each object in turn. Ask: does it show all seven signs of life right now?
If it shows all seven, place it in Living. If it never had life, place it in Non-living.
If it does not show the signs now but was once part of a living thing (like wood or cloth), place it in Once-living.
For each object, write one reason for your choice.

activities.title: A Leaf Hunt and Sorting Challenge
activities.materials: Four to six different leaves you can safely pick or find fallen (for example acacia, mango, maize, aloe vera, grass, a flowering shrub); A notebook, pencil and a ruler
steps: Lay all your leaves side by side on a clean surface.
Choose one feature - for example, 'Is the leaf long and narrow?' - and split the leaves into two piles: yes and no.
For each pile, choose another feature and split again.
Keep going until every leaf is separated from all the others.
Record which feature you used at each split.

activities.title: Build Your Own Dichotomous Key
activities.materials: Four different specimens: either four insects you have observed (housefly, ant, beetle, grasshopper) or four leaves from Investigation 2; Notebook and pencil
steps: Observe all four specimens carefully and list the features of each (number of legs, wings, size, colour, body shape).
Find one feature that splits the four into two groups of two. Write it as statement 1a and 1b.
For each group of two, find a feature that separates them. Write these as statements 2 and 3.
Each statement must end either in 'go to ...' or in the name of the organism.
Write your finished key neatly using numbered pairs.

activities.title: Modelling a Virus
activities.materials: A ball of soft dough (flour and water) or clay, or a scrunched ball of paper; Small pieces of thread, wool or torn paper (to be the RNA inside); Toothpicks, matchsticks or small dry twigs (to be the spikes) - handle carefully; A small clear bag or bowl to hold the model
steps: Form the dough or paper into a round ball. This is the protein coat.
Before closing the ball, tuck a few short threads inside. These are the RNA instructions.
Push the toothpicks or twigs gently into the outside of the ball to make the spikes.
Look at your model. Label a drawing of it in your notebook: protein coat, RNA, spikes.

activities.title: Observe: Grouping and Identifying Organisms — part 5
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of grouping and identifying organisms — part 5 around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Grouping and Identifying Organisms — part 6
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of grouping and identifying organisms — part 6 around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Eat other organisms; usually move; many cells.
Make food by photosynthesis; usually green.
Feed on other material; reproduce by spores.
Single tiny cells with no nucleus.
A permanent increase in size, built from within.
Making more organisms of the same species.
Removing waste materials made inside the body.
Taking in nutrients for energy, growth and repair.
One of the largest groups into which living things are sorted.

vocabulary.example: Think of a kingdom as the biggest box on the shelf.

vocabulary.meaning: Eat other organisms; usually move; many cells.
vocabulary.example: Construct your own dichotomous key to identify a small set of local animals, leaves or objects.

vocabulary.meaning: Make food by photosynthesis; usually green.
vocabulary.example: East Africa alone is home to lions, elephants, giraffes, camels, tilapia in the Jubba River, coral fish along the Indian Ocean coast, and countless insects, plants and tiny microbes.

vocabulary.meaning: Feed on other material; reproduce by spores.
vocabulary.example: A single teaspoon of healthy soil from a farm near the Jubba River can contain more living organisms - mostly bacteria and fungi - than there are people in the whole world.

vocabulary.meaning: Single tiny cells with no nucleus.
vocabulary.example: A single teaspoon of healthy soil from a farm near the Jubba River can contain more living organisms - mostly bacteria and fungi - than there are people in the whole world.

vocabulary.meaning: A permanent increase in size, built from within.
vocabulary.example: Movement and growth are characteristics of life.

vocabulary.meaning: Making more organisms of the same species.
Removing waste materials made inside the body.
Taking in nutrients for energy, growth and repair.
vocabulary.example: Animals such as lions and humans get their nutrition by eating other organisms.

vocabulary.meaning: One of the largest groups into which living things are sorted.
vocabulary.example: Think of a kingdom as the biggest box on the shelf.
I can explain the difference between a vertebrate and an invertebrate.

commonMistakes: Movement is only one sign; cars and rivers move too.
Breathing is moving air; respiration is inside cells.
Bacteria are living cells; viruses are not cells.
Ligers are infertile and only occur in captivity.

connections.text: Cells (Unit 1): all living things are made of cells - except viruses, which are not. This unit builds directly on what a cell is.
Food chains and ecology (later units): classifying organisms into producers, herbivores and carnivores depends on the grouping skills you learned here.
understanding viruses connects to real life - preventing measles, flu and COVID-19 through cleanliness and vaccination.
the kingdoms and species of East Africa link to habitats like the savanna, the Rift Valley lakes and the Indian Ocean coast.
building and reading keys uses clear logical thinking - the same 'if yes... if no...' reasoning used in problem solving.
Start a nature notebook. Each week, observe and sketch one new organism near your home and note which kingdom it belongs to.

realProblems.prompt: Amina finds a small creature with eight legs and no wings near her home in Hargeisa. Using the idea of a key, what question would help her decide if it is an insect or a spider? What is your reasoning?
realProblems.answer: A good question is 'Does it have six legs?' (or 'Does it have eight legs?'). Insects have six legs; spiders have eight. Since the creature has eight legs, it is a spider, not an insect.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A good question is 'Does it have six legs?' (or 'Does it have eight legs?'). Insects have six legs; spiders have eight. Since the creature has eight legs, it is a spider, not an insect.

realProblems.prompt: During a measles outbreak, Farah's little brother is unwell. Explain, using what you know about how viruses replicate, why keeping him at home and washing hands protects the rest of the family.
realProblems.answer: A virus can only make copies inside a living cell and spreads from person to person. Keeping the brother at home stops the virus reaching others, and washing hands removes particles before they spread. Together this breaks the chain of infection.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A virus can only make copies inside a living cell and spreads from person to person. Keeping the brother at home stops the virus reaching others, and washing hands removes particles before they spread. Together this breaks the chain of infection.

realProblems.prompt: A farmer near the Jubba River has two herds of cattle that look slightly different. How could he find out whether they are the same species?
realProblems.answer: He should see whether the two herds reproduce together to produce fertile offspring. If they breed freely and their calves can also breed, they are the same species. Checking many pairs makes the conclusion more certain.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: He should see whether the two herds reproduce together to produce fertile offspring. If they breed freely and their calves can also breed, they are the same species. Checking many pairs makes the conclusion more certain.

realProblems.prompt: Use this key to identify a housefly: 1a has legs (go to 2), 1b no legs (worm); 2a six legs (go to 3), 2b more than six legs (crab); 3a two wings (housefly), 3b four wings (dragonfly). Write out the path.
realProblems.answer: Path: 1a (has legs) go to 2; 2a (six legs) go to 3; 3a (two wings) = housefly. The path is 1a, 2a, 3a.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Path: 1a (has legs) go to 2; 2a (six legs) go to 3; 3a (two wings) = housefly. The path is 1a, 2a, 3a.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf wants to build a key for four leaves: acacia, mango, maize and aloe vera. Suggest a good first question that would split them into two groups of two.
realProblems.answer: A good first question is 'Is the leaf long and narrow?' This puts maize and aloe vera in one group and acacia and mango in the other - two groups of two.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A good first question is 'Is the leaf long and narrow?' This puts maize and aloe vera in one group and acacia and mango in the other - two groups of two.

realProblems.prompt: A scientist argues that viruses should count as living because they reproduce. Another argues they are not living. Give the strongest point for each side, then say which you find more convincing and why.
realProblems.answer: For alive: viruses reproduce (replicate) and carry genetic instructions like living things. Against: on their own they show none of the other six characteristics and cannot do anything without a host cell. A good answer picks one side and gives a clear reason, e.g. 'not alive, because it cannot…
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: For alive: viruses reproduce (replicate) and carry genetic instructions like living things. Against: on their own they show none of the other six characteristics and cannot do anything without a host cell. A good answer picks one side and gives a clear reason, e.g. 'not alive, because it cannot…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: List the seven characteristics of living organisms.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition (MRS GREN). Each is a sign of life.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between respiration and breathing.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Breathing is moving air in and out of the body. Respiration happens inside every cell, breaking down food to release energy. Plants respire but do not breathe.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give one example each of a living, a non-living, and a once-living object from your home.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Answers vary. Example: living = a potted plant; non-living = a stone; once-living = a wooden spoon (it came from a tree).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a vertebrate and an invertebrate? Give one local example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A vertebrate has a backbone (e.g. tilapia or camel); an invertebrate has none (e.g. a mosquito or scorpion).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe the three main parts of a virus.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A virus has a protein coat on the outside, spikes/pegs sticking out of it, and RNA (genetic instructions) inside. It is not made of cells.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Define the word species in your own words.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A species is a group of organisms that share the same features and can reproduce together to produce fertile offspring.

questions.question: How many characteristics of life do all living organisms share?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: seven. All living things share the seven MRS GREN characteristics.

questions.question: Breaking down food inside cells to release energy is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: respiration. Respiration releases energy from food inside every cell.

questions.question: Which of these is an invertebrate?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: mosquito. A mosquito has no backbone, so it is an invertebrate. The others are vertebrates.

questions.question: A virus's outer coat is made of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: protein. The outer coat of a virus is made of protein, with spikes on it.

questions.question: Getting rid of waste made inside the body is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: excretion. Excretion is removing waste made inside the body, such as carbon dioxide.

questions.question: Which instrument is needed to see a virus?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: an electron microscope. Viruses are far too small for a school light microscope.

questions.question: A group of organisms that can reproduce to make fertile offspring is a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: species. A species is defined by the ability to produce fertile offspring together.

questions.question: The offspring of a lion and a tiger (a liger) is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: infertile. Ligers are healthy but cannot have young, so they are infertile.

questions.question: 'Dichotomous' means:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: branching into two. At each step a dichotomous key offers exactly two choices.

questions.question: Which is the best question for a dichotomous key?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Does it have six legs? It has a clear yes-or-no answer everyone would agree on.

questions.question: Green plants get their nutrition by:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: photosynthesis. Green plants make their own food using sunlight, water and air.

questions.question: Which kingdom does a mushroom belong to?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: fungi. Mushrooms belong to the fungi kingdom; they do not photosynthesise.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can list and describe the seven characteristics shared by all living organisms.
I can decide whether an object is living, non-living, or was once alive, giving reasons.
I can describe the main groups (kingdoms) into which living things are classified.
I can explain the difference between vertebrates and invertebrates and give local examples of each.
I can describe the structure of a virus and discuss why scientists argue about whether viruses are alive.
I can define the word 'species' and explain how scientists decide if two organisms belong to the same species.
I can use a dichotomous key to identify an organism, working through it step by step.
